1: hoy ir damim — "Woe to the bloody city!" Ir damim (city of blood, plural — intensive) is the woe-oracle directed at the city as a whole. Three characteristics: full of lies (kalah sheker), full of plunder (perekh), where the prey never departs. The triple accumulation (blood, deception, violence) names the three modalities of Nineveh's imperial practice: military slaughter, diplomatic deception, and economic extraction.
2: qol shot uqol ra'ash ofan — "The crack of the whip, the rumble of the wheel." An onomatopoeic battle scene: the sounds of Nineveh's approaching (or now attacking) army turned against itself — whip cracks, wheel rumble, galloping horses, bouncing chariots (merkavah menateret). The war-machine sounds that once signaled conquest now signal defeat.
3: parash me'aleh velahav cherev — "The horseman charges, the flashing sword and glittering spear." The battle continues: mass casualties, no end to the corpses, they stumble over their dead. The vivid present tense puts the reader in the city during its fall. The city that turned its military power against others now experiences the same sword, the same stumbling over corpses that marked Assyrian conquest campaigns.
4: merov zanunei zona tovat chen — "Because of the many prostitutions of the prostitute, the graceful and deadly enchantress." The metaphor shifts: Nineveh as a prostitute-enchantress (ba'alat kishufim, mistress of sorceries) who seduced nations into alliance and then enslaved them. The selling of nations through her prostitutions (zanuneiha) and families through her sorceries is the language of political-economic exploitation described as sexual commerce. The city's seductive power over subject nations is named as prostitution.
5: hineni eleyikh ne'um YHWH tzva'ot — "Behold, I am against you, declares YHWH of hosts." The second "I am against you" (cf. Nahum 2:13) frames the woe oracle. The punishment fits the crime: as Nineveh shamed nations through her prostitutions, YHWH will lift her skirts over her face (giliti she'ulayikh al-panayikh) and show the nations her nakedness. The humiliation is public — the nations who were once enslaved to her will now see her exposed.
6: ve-hashlikhothi aleyikh shiqutzim — "I will throw filth at you and make you vile." The reversal of Nineveh's dignity: the city that demanded respect and tribute will be treated as dung (shiqutzim, detestable things) and made into a spectacle. All who look at her will flee from her — the revulsion is universal. The empire that attracted envoys and tribute delegations from across the world will repel all observers.
7: ve-hayah kol ro'ayikh yiddod mimekh — "All who see you will recoil from you." The rhetorical question: "Who will grieve for Nineveh? Where shall I seek comforters for her?" (me-ayan avakesh menachemim lakh). The absence of comforters (menachemim) is the ultimate social isolation — not even sympathy is available. The word menachemim echoes Lamentations 1's repeated refrain about Jerusalem having no comforter. The poetic justice: Nineveh, who showed no comfort to the nations it destroyed, will find none when destroyed.
8: hatov mino-amon ha-yoshevet be-yeor — "Are you better than No-amon (Thebes), which was seated by the Nile?" The rhetorical comparison: Thebes, the great Egyptian city, was sacked by Assyria in 663 BCE. Thebes had the Nile, water on every side, allies in Cush and Egypt and Put and Libya — and she still fell. If Nineveh destroyed Thebes, what makes Nineveh think it is exempt? The empire's own actions provide the precedent for its own judgment.
9: Kush atzmatah ve-Mitzrayim — "Cush was her strength, and Egypt too, without limit." Thebes had formidable allies: Cush (Nubia), Egypt, Put (Libya), and the Lubim — all part of her defensive coalition. Yet she went into exile (galah galtah), her children were dashed to pieces at the corner of every street, her nobles were divided by lot, her great men were bound in chains. If the city with all these allies could fall, so can Nineveh.
10: gam hi le-gola halkhah — "She also went into exile, into captivity." Thebes' fall is rehearsed: her young children dashed to pieces (rutashu), her nobles (nikhbaddeiha) cast lots for as booty, her great men bound in chains. The parallel between Thebes' fate and Nineveh's imminent fate is the core of the rhetorical argument: Nineveh did this to others; it will happen to Nineveh.
11: gam at tishkeri tih'i ne'alamah — "You also will be drunk; you will be hidden." The intoxication metaphor for judgment (cf. Jer 25; Habakkuk 2:16) — the cup of YHWH's wrath makes the nation stagger. "You will seek a refuge from the enemy (meqoz me-oyev)." The city that was the source of terror will desperately seek safety from what it once visited on others.
12: kol-mibtza'yikh teinim im-bikurim — "All your fortresses are like fig trees with early figs." The simile: when they are shaken, the figs fall into the mouth of the eater. Nineveh's fortifications, which took generations to build, will yield to the conqueror as easily as ripe figs fall from a tree when shaken. The fortress system that seemed impregnable proves as fragile as fruit.
13: hineh amekh nashim be-qirbekh — "Your troops are women in your midst." The taunt: Nineveh's warriors have become women — the ancient shame-rhetoric for military failure. The gates of your land are wide open to your enemies; fire consumes your gate-bars. The gate-system (the most critical element of ancient urban defense) is consumed, leaving the city exposed.
14: shevi lakh mayim matzor — "Draw water for the siege." Nahum ironically commands Nineveh to prepare for siege: draw water, strengthen your fortifications, go into the clay and tread the mortar, take hold of the brick mold. The defensive preparations are futile — named not as effective strategy but as the desperate futility of a city whose end is already determined.
15: sham tochalekh esh takhritakh cherev — "There fire will consume you, the sword will cut you off." The futility climax: despite all preparations, fire and sword end the city. Then the second ironic taunt: "Multiply yourselves like the locust, multiply like the swarming locust!" The command to multiply is futile — it echoes the locust imagery of Nahum's day. The word arbeh (locust) dominates Nahum 3:15-17.
16: hirbeit rokhalayikh mikkokevei hashamayim — "You increased your merchants more than the stars of heaven." Nineveh's commercial empire is described: merchants more than stars. Then: the locust sheds its skin and flies away. The merchant empire is as temporary as the locust — present in overwhelming numbers, then suddenly absent. The commercial infrastructure of the empire vanishes as completely as the locust swarm at dawn.
17: minzarayikh ke-arbeh — "Your princes are like locusts, your scribes like swarms of locusts." The administrative apparatus of empire — princes (minzarim, a rare word; possibly cognate with Assyrian governors) and scribes/officials — is compared to locusts that camp in the hedges on a cold day, then fly off when the sun rises. "No one knows where they are" — the empire's bureaucracy evaporates when the empire falls. The officials who organized the tribute system scatter as completely as locusts.
18: namu ro'ayikh melek Ashur — "Your shepherds are asleep, O king of Assyria; your nobles slumber." The king is directly addressed for the first time explicitly. His "shepherds" (ro'im = the leaders responsible for the people) are asleep — the image of leaders who have failed in their watchman role. The people are scattered on the mountains with no one to gather them. The shepherd imagery (pastoral care, gathering the flock) inverted: Assyria's own people are scattered, ungathered, on the mountains.
19: ein kehavah le-shibbrekha — "There is no healing of your wound; your injury is fatal (nakhla makkatkha)." The final verdict: no healing, no remedy. All who hear the news of Nineveh's fall will clap their hands over you — not in grief but in relief and vindication: "For upon whom has not your wickedness passed continually?" The universal experience of Assyrian cruelty becomes the explanation for universal rejoicing at its fall. The book ends not with lament but with the relief of a world released from a predator.
